questions.q: Ānāpānasati organizes the practice into how many steps, in how many tetrads?
questions.a: Sixteen steps, arranged in four tetrads of four. The first tetrad is body, the second feelings, the third mind, the fourth mental qualities — which maps ānāpānasati onto the Four Foundations of Mindfulness (MN 10). One practice, fulfilling the whole satipaṭṭhāna structure.

questions.q: The first two instructions are deceptively simple. What are they, and why does the tradition take them so seriously?
questions.a: "Breathing in long, one knows 'I breathe in long.' Breathing out long, one knows 'I breathe out long.' Breathing in short, one knows 'I breathe in short'..." — there is no attempt to control the breath, only to know it as it is. This trains the base faculty that everything else depends on: the capacity to know what is happening as it happens.

questions.q: The third tetrad moves to the mind (citta). What three qualities does the practitioner train themselves to experience?
questions.a: Gladdening the mind (abhippamodayaṃ), concentrating the mind (samādahaṃ), liberating the mind (vimocayaṃ). These are not mental states to grasp at — they are natural results of the first two tetrads done well.

questions.q: What is the relationship between ānāpānasati and the Seven Factors of Awakening (bojjhaṅgā)?
questions.a: The sutta explicitly states that ānāpānasati developed fulfills satipaṭṭhāna; satipaṭṭhāna developed fulfills the seven factors; the seven factors developed fulfill clear knowing and liberation. It is a complete path whose root is this one practice.

questions.q: The sutta was taught on a specific occasion. What was the occasion?
questions.a: The end of a rains retreat, to a community of monks who had been practicing intensively together. The Buddha did not leave — he stayed another month because the group was ready for this teaching. This detail matters: ānāpānasati is presented as a fulfilling teaching for people already established in practice, not a technique for distraction.

questions.q: What is the honest difference between "just watching the breath" and ānāpānasati as the Buddha teaches it here?
questions.a: Watching the breath can be a concentration exercise. Ānāpānasati uses the breath as the base but progresses through feelings, mind, and mental qualities — developing the full satipaṭṭhāna structure and the seven factors of awakening. It is contemplation of the whole person, with the breath as the continuous thread.
